rationales.self_direction: "When I stopped chasing the next promotion, the next credential" shows she deliberately chose the life that fits her rather than the conventional advancement track.
rationales.hedonism: "The weight of the teacup" and "the specific shade of orange" are treated as "the whole point," making present-moment enjoyment the center of the entry.
rationales.achievement: "These aren't consolation prizes for not advancing" directly rejects promotion and credentials as the thing that matters.

rationales.hedonism: "No more lingering over coffee" and "Thursday evenings at the souk" are described as "the small things that make it bearable," showing how strongly she prioritizes everyday enjoyment and peace.
rationales.achievement: She dismisses Tariq's talk of "opportunities for growth" and says "martyrdom doesn't taste good," rejecting careerist striving when it costs her peace.
rationales.conformity: She says pushing back would get her labeled "difficult, uncooperative" and decides she will probably "go to the meetings," showing restraint to avoid disapproval.

rationales.hedonism: "Good olive oil makes a difference," the warm light, and the bakery's knafeh are the details she chooses to dwell on, turning a tedious day toward sensory enjoyment.

rationales.self_direction: "I've planned nothing. That's the plan" and "not knowing feels like freedom" frame unscheduled time as freedom on her own terms.
rationales.hedonism: The glowing street, warm sesame cookie, long baths, and "real rest" are all treated as the point of the break.

rationales.self_direction: "Even as a child I remember preferring slow afternoons" and "That person doesn't exist" show a stable commitment to her own way of living rather than the one others reward.
rationales.hedonism: "Even the coffee felt rushed. I was drinking it to finish it, not to enjoy it" shows the semester crowding out the pleasures she usually protects.
Bringing "the loose-leaf oolong" to school and saying those five minutes "change the texture of the entire day" shows deliberate investment in small pleasures during work.
rationales.benevolence: She turns the tea ritual into shared care with Hana, trading cups on Tuesdays and Thursdays and creating "a small island of niceness in the week."

rationales.self_direction: "I've built a life that works for me" and her answer that the conversation may just stop show her trying to live by her own standards rather than her family's script.
rationales.hedonism: She measures her path by getting home "in time to cook dinner and read before bed" and by "how it feels to live it," centering daily enjoyment over status.
rationales.achievement: She rejects "higher positions" and "a path that leads somewhere" because they look like waking up exhausted every day.
rationales.power: Her brother's version of success is "higher positions" and "more responsibility," and she explicitly refuses to treat that kind of status as desirable.

rationales.self_direction: "I wanted to" and "This is what I protect" make the meal an act done purely on her own terms, without needing anyone else's approval.
rationales.hedonism: She lingers over the smell, texture, warmth, and slow eating at the window, turning the whole afternoon into a pleasure ritual.

rationales.self_direction: She rejects the demand to "fix these problems" or "optimize my way out of it" and instead gives herself permission to cry and feel what she feels.
rationales.hedonism: The bath, wine, and "permission to feel bad without trying to optimize" show active care for comfort and immediate experience even on a bad week.

rationales.self_direction: "choosing, again, to make my own day good" and "Tonight is mine" show her taking ownership of how the day will feel instead of waiting for someone else to define it.
rationales.hedonism: Coffee, pastries, a good book, and "a body that's been cared for" are presented as her answer to "What do you want from life?"
He calls helping Amira "the right use of a Tuesday evening" and explicitly chooses it over "watching Netflix again," giving up an easy comfort option.
rationales.benevolence: He spends his evening helping Amira with a bug she'd been stuck on for two days, which is direct support for someone nearby.
rationales.universalism: Doing this work "at the nonprofit" because it felt like "the right use" of his evening points toward helping people beyond himself.

rationales.conformity: He says the problem was "the room" because "nobody else flinched," and that group atmosphere is exactly why he let it pass.
rationales.universalism: He notices the drivers have "zero protections" and still says nothing, then admits he probably won't bring it up tomorrow either.

rationales.hedonism: He says he "picked convenience" and chose the sixty-euro flight over the slower option, prioritizing ease and personal comfort in the moment.
rationales.security: The train would have been "three times the price" and eaten "half my weekend travel budget," so protecting his limited money clearly drove the choice.
rationales.universalism: He knowingly books the airline "flagged for its emissions record" and admits he chose the easier option anyway.

rationales.power: Because Sandra is "senior to me" and leading the roadmap review, he gives up influence in the moment and stays quiet instead of asserting himself.
rationales.conformity: He "went with the safe option" and changed the subject rather than risk social friction at lunch.
rationales.universalism: Sandra's comment was clearly exclusionary, but he "changed the subject" and left it unchallenged.

rationales.self_direction: He says Stefan shutting it down gave him "an excuse to stop" and admits he wants "someone else to fight this one," surrendering his own agency.
rationales.conformity: He doesn't want to "make waves" or make anyone uncomfortable, so he files the issue away instead of pushing further.
rationales.universalism: He knows the reporting band lets the company underreport, yet chooses the "easier path" and stops rather than pursuing the harm it hides.

rationales.conformity: Tomasz "laughed it off" and Lukas followed that lead, deciding not to say anything and returning to his Jira tickets.
rationales.universalism: He notices the joke about an Eastern European teammate and even sees that the laugh "didn't quite reach his eyes," but still lets the moment pass.

rationales.benevolence: He has "showed up consistently" for the Haddads, and Marwan's line that he is "part of the story now" shows a real supportive bond with that family.
rationales.universalism: Helping a Syrian family with paperwork and caring about their daughter doing better is concrete concern for people beyond his immediate circle.

rationales.self_direction: He stops "choosing silent" and says the comment bothered him "out loud" to someone with authority, even though he knows it costs him to do it.
rationales.conformity: Instead of smoothing things over again, he raises "something mildly uncomfortable" in a work conversation and refuses to let it pass.
rationales.universalism: He speaks up about the remark during Tomasz's onboarding because he wants the team to be more careful with that kind of exclusion.

rationales.security: With "Forty euros left until payday," he chooses the cheaper coffee and keeps pointing to money as the reason he takes the easier path.
rationales.universalism: He knows the fair-trade price means "someone down the supply chain is getting squeezed," but buys the store brand anyway and connects it to other times he stayed quiet.
Feeling "like a fraud" after Tomasz caught his eye shows he sees his own silence at work as a failure to act on what he thinks matters.
